name: Glycogen synthesis and breakdown (glycogenesis & glycogenolysis)
summary: Storage and mobilization of glucose as glycogen: UDP-glucose-driven chain elongation and branching (glycogenesis) versus phosphorolytic and hydrolytic breakdown (glycogenolysis), under reciprocal hormonal and covalent control.
description: Glycogen is a highly branched α-1,4/α-1,6 glucose polymer that buffers blood glucose (liver) and provides rapid on-site fuel (skeletal muscle). **Glycogenesis:** glucose 6-phosphate is isomerized to glucose 1-phosphate (phosphoglucomutase), activated to UDP-glucose (UDP-glucose pyrophosphorylase, driven forward by pyrophosphate hydrolysis), then transferred to a growing chain — first onto the self-priming protein glycogenin, then elongated by glycogen synthase (the committed, regulated step) and branched by branching enzyme. **Glycogenolysis:** glycogen phosphorylase (the committed step, PLP-dependent) removes α-1,4 residues as glucose 1-phosphate, and the bifunctional debranching enzyme handles α-1,6 branch points, releasing a small fraction as free glucose. Glucose 1-phosphate rejoins the hexose-phosphate pool via phosphoglucomutase; in liver and kidney, glucose-6-phosphatase then releases free glucose to the blood. The two directions are never fully active at once: an insulin arm (PP1 activation, GSK-3 inhibition) favors synthesis, while a glucagon/epinephrine → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylase-kinase cascade, plus AMP/Ca2+ allosteric inputs, favors breakdown.

references.title: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8th ed. (Nelson & Cox), Ch. 15
Berg, Tymoczko, Gatto, Stryer — Biochemistry, 9th ed., Ch. 21: Glycogen Metabolism
Glycogen and its metabolism: some new developments and old themes

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Glycogen granules and all glycogen-metabolizing enzymes except glucose-6-phosphatase reside here.

compartments.name: Endoplasmic reticulum
compartments.description: The glucose-6-phosphatase catalytic site faces the ER lumen; hepatic/renal free-glucose production occurs here.

metabolites.name: Glycogen
metabolites.description: Branched α-1,4-linked glucose polymer with α-1,6 branch points every ~8-12 residues.

metabolites.name: Glucagon
metabolites.description: 29-residue pancreatic α-cell peptide hormone that signals the fasted state to the liver.

metabolites.name: Insulin
metabolites.description: Pancreatic β-cell peptide hormone that signals the fed state and promotes glycogen storage.

enzymes.name: Phosphoglucomutase-1
enzymes.description: Interconverts glucose 1-phosphate and glucose 6-phosphate through a glucose 1,6-bisphosphate intermediate; shared entry/exit valve for both glycogenesis and glycogenolysis.

enzymes.name: UTP--glucose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (UDP-glucose pyrophosphorylase)
enzymes.description: Activates glucose 1-phosphate to UDP-glucose, the universal glucosyl donor.

enzymes.name: Inorganic pyrophosphatase 1
enzymes.description: Hydrolyzes the pyrophosphate released by UDP-glucose synthesis, rendering glycogenesis thermodynamically irreversible.

enzymes.name: Glycogenin-1
enzymes.description: Self-glucosylates Tyr194 to build the ~8-12 residue α-1,4 primer on which glycogen synthase elongates; remains covalently attached at the core of each glycogen granule.

enzymes.name: Glycogen synthase, muscle
enzymes.description: Elongates the non-reducing ends of glycogen with α-1,4-glucosyl units from UDP-glucose; the committed and most heavily regulated step of glycogen synthesis.

enzymes.name: Glycogen synthase, liver
enzymes.description: Liver isozyme of glycogen synthase.

enzymes.name: 1,4-alpha-glucan branching enzyme
enzymes.description: Transfers ~6-7 residue α-1,4 segments to form α-1,6 branch points, increasing solubility and the number of non-reducing ends for rapid synthesis/degradation.

enzymes.name: Glycogen phosphorylase, muscle form (myophosphorylase)
enzymes.description: Rate-limiting enzyme of glycogenolysis; the essential PLP 5'-phosphate acts as a general acid/base in phosphorolysis. Controlled by both covalent (b/a) and allosteric (AMP, ATP, G6P) mechanisms.

enzymes.name: Glycogen phosphorylase, liver form
enzymes.description: Liver isozyme; phosphorylase a is directly inhibited by glucose, making it a glucose sensor rather than an energy-charge sensor.

enzymes.name: Glycogen phosphorylase, brain form
enzymes.description: Brain/fetal isozyme of glycogen phosphorylase.

enzymes.name: Glycogen debranching enzyme (bifunctional 4-alpha-glucanotransferase / amylo-alpha-1,6-glucosidase)
enzymes.description: Single polypeptide with two active sites: the transferase relocates a maltotriosyl unit off a branch, then the glucosidase hydrolyzes the exposed α-1,6 bond, releasing free glucose.

enzymes.name: Glucose-6-phosphatase catalytic subunit 1
enzymes.description: ER-membrane enzyme of liver and kidney; hydrolyzes glucose 6-phosphate to free glucose for export to blood. Absent from muscle, which therefore cannot release glucose.

enzymes.name: cAMP-dependent protein kinase catalytic subunit alpha (PKA C-alpha)
enzymes.description: Released from its regulatory subunits by cAMP; phosphorylates phosphorylase kinase (activating) and glycogen synthase (inactivating).

enzymes.name: Phosphorylase b kinase regulatory subunit alpha, muscle isoform
enzymes.description: Regulatory α-subunit of the phosphorylase kinase holoenzyme; a target of PKA phosphorylation.

enzymes.name: Phosphorylase b kinase regulatory subunit beta
enzymes.description: Regulatory β-subunit of phosphorylase kinase; its PKA phosphorylation is the principal activating event of the holoenzyme.

enzymes.name: Phosphorylase b kinase catalytic subunit gamma-1, skeletal muscle
enzymes.description: Catalytic γ-subunit of phosphorylase kinase; phosphorylates glycogen phosphorylase Ser14.

enzymes.name: Calmodulin-1 (phosphorylase kinase delta subunit)
enzymes.description: Integral δ-subunit of phosphorylase kinase; binding of Ca2+ activates the holoenzyme, coupling muscle contraction to glycogenolysis.

enzymes.name: Glycogen synthase kinase-3 beta
enzymes.description: Constitutively active kinase that inactivates glycogen synthase by multisite phosphorylation; inhibited downstream of insulin/PKB signaling.

enzymes.name: 5'-AMP-activated protein kinase catalytic subunit alpha-2 (AMPK)
enzymes.description: Energy-sensing kinase (activated by high AMP/ATP) that phosphorylates and inactivates glycogen synthase, sparing ATP.

enzymes.name: Serine/threonine-protein phosphatase PP1-alpha catalytic subunit
enzymes.description: Catalytic subunit of protein phosphatase 1; when targeted to glycogen it activates glycogen synthase and inactivates phosphorylase and phosphorylase kinase.

enzymes.name: Protein phosphatase 1 regulatory subunit 3A (G_M / R_GL glycogen-targeting subunit)
enzymes.description: Scaffolds PP1 catalytic subunit onto the glycogen particle; an insulin-responsive node that biases PP1 toward the glycogen substrates.

complexes.name: Phosphorylase kinase (αβγδ)4 holoenzyme
complexes.description: 1.3 MDa (αβγδ)4 complex. γ is catalytic, δ (calmodulin) is the Ca2+ sensor, and α/β are the regulatory subunits phosphorylated by PKA. Integrates hormonal (PKA) and neural/contractile (Ca2+) signals onto glycogen phosphorylase.

complexes.name: Glycogen-associated protein phosphatase 1 (PP1-G_M)
complexes.description: PP1 catalytic subunit targeted to the glycogen particle by PPP1R3A (G_M/R_GL). Reverses the PKA cascade: activates glycogen synthase and inactivates phosphorylase and phosphorylase kinase.

provenance.notes: Bidirectional link between the hexose-phosphate pool and glycogen; runs toward G1P in synthesis and toward G6P in breakdown.
The reaction itself is freely reversible; net UDP-glucose synthesis is pulled forward by pyrophosphatase hydrolysis of the released PPi.
Strongly exergonic PPi hydrolysis makes the UDP-glucose step effectively irreversible in vivo.
Glycogenin self-glucosylates Tyr194 to build the ~8-12 residue α-1,4 primer (modeled here as nascent glycogen). The protein acceptor is intentionally omitted from the small-molecule balance.

reactions.name: Glycogen synthase (chain elongation) — committed step of glycogenesis

provenance.notes: Adds α-1,4-glucosyl units to non-reducing ends; effectively irreversible because UDP-glucose is a high-energy donor. KEGG R00292 models the glucan as amylose (C00718); the human substrate/product is glycogen (C00182).
Intramolecular transfer of ~6-7 residue segments from α-1,4 chains to α-1,6 branch points; no net small-molecule change. KEGG R02110 models the glucans as amylose→starch.

reactions.name: Glycogen phosphorylase (phosphorolysis) — committed step of glycogenolysis

provenance.notes: Sequentially releases α-1,4 residues as glucose 1-phosphate, stopping ~4 residues from a branch (the limit dextrin). Chemically reversible but driven toward degradation in vivo by the high cellular [Pi]/[G1P] ratio.

Bifunctional AGL: 4-α-glucanotransferase (EC 2.4.1.25) relocates a maltotriosyl unit, then amylo-α-1,6-glucosidase (EC 3.2.1.33) hydrolyzes the α-1,6 bond, releasing ~1 free glucose per branch (~8-9% of glycogen glucose).
Liver/kidney only; ER-lumen hydrolysis releases free glucose to the blood. Muscle lacks G6Pase and retains glucose 6-phosphate for glycolysis. Shared with gluconeogenesis.

regulations.mechanism: Glucose 6-phosphate binds glycogen synthase allosterically, activating it (especially the phosphorylated synthase-D form) and promoting its dephosphorylation by PP1 — substrate availability directly favors storage.
Glucose 6-phosphate binds phosphorylase a, stabilizing the inactive T state — reinforcing reciprocal control with glycogen synthase.
AMP binds the nucleotide site of muscle phosphorylase b, shifting it toward the active R state — links a low energy charge to glycogen breakdown independently of the covalent b→a conversion.
ATP (together with glucose 6-phosphate) competes with AMP for the nucleotide site, holding phosphorylase b in the inactive T state when the energy charge is high.

In liver, free glucose binds phosphorylase a, promoting the T state and exposing Ser14-phosphate to PP1; this makes hepatic phosphorylase a a direct glucose sensor that shuts down glycogenolysis when blood glucose is high.
Ca2+ binds the calmodulin δ-subunit of phosphorylase kinase, activating the holoenzyme; couples the Ca2+ transient of muscle contraction to immediate glycogen mobilization.
PKA phosphorylates the α and β regulatory subunits of phosphorylase kinase (β is the principal activating site), converting it to the active form during the hormone → cAMP → PKA cascade.
Phosphorylase kinase phosphorylates Ser14 of glycogen phosphorylase, converting inactive phosphorylase b to active phosphorylase a.
PKA phosphorylates glycogen synthase at multiple serines, inactivating it at the same time it activates glycogenolysis — the reciprocal core of the covalent switch.
GSK-3 sequentially phosphorylates C-terminal sites 3a-3c (after CK2 priming at site 5), the dominant inactivating modification of glycogen synthase.
AMPK, activated by a high AMP/ATP ratio, phosphorylates glycogen synthase (site 2) and inactivates it, preventing ATP-costly glycogen synthesis when energy is scarce.
Glycogen-associated PP1 dephosphorylates glycogen synthase, converting it to the active (a) form — the dominant activation arm downstream of insulin.
PP1 dephosphorylates Ser14 of phosphorylase a, returning it to inactive phosphorylase b and shutting down breakdown.
PP1 dephosphorylates phosphorylase kinase, returning it to the low-activity state and reinforcing the off-switch for glycogenolysis.
cAMP binds the regulatory (R) subunits of protein kinase A, releasing the active catalytic (C) subunits.
Glucagon (fasting; liver) activates a Gs-coupled receptor → adenylate cyclase → cAMP → PKA, driving glycogenolysis and suppressing glycogen synthesis to raise blood glucose.
Epinephrine (β-adrenergic; muscle and liver) raises cAMP and activates PKA, mobilizing glycogen during stress and exercise.
Insulin → PI3K → PKB/Akt phosphorylates and inhibits GSK-3, relieving inhibitory phosphorylation of glycogen synthase (with parallel PP1 activation) to promote glycogen storage after a meal.

relations.note: Liver (GYS2) and muscle/ubiquitous (GYS1) glycogen synthase isozymes.
Liver (PYGL) vs muscle (PYGM) phosphorylase: the liver form is a glucose sensor, the muscle form an AMP/energy-charge sensor.
Muscle glycogenolysis feeds glucose 6-phosphate directly into glycolysis, bypassing the hexokinase ATP cost.
Gluconeogenic glucose 6-phosphate can be routed into glycogen; both pathways share glucose-6-phosphatase for hepatic glucose output.
Glucose 6-phosphate is the shared entry metabolite of the pentose phosphate pathway.
Hepatic glucose-6-phosphatase releases free glucose to the blood, fueling glycolysis in peripheral tissues.
